coredata.dc:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose-based functional film integrated with chitosan-based carbon quantum dots for active food packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Riahi, Zohreh
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Bagheri, Reza
Pircheraghi, Gholamreza
Lotfali, Ensieh
coredata.dc:description: A chitosan-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multifunctional properties were synthesized using a hydrothermal process. The CQDs were spherical with an average size of 7.8 ± 2.4 nm. The CQD showed strong antioxidant activity, potent antibacterial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogenes, and antifungal activity against A. niger and P. chrysogenum. In addition, the prepared CQD showed negligible cytotoxicity against L929 cells even at a concentration of 500 μg/mL. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-based functional films were fabricated by adding various amounts of CQD. The CQDs were evenly dispersed in the polymer matrix to form a highly transparent UV-blocking film. The addition of CQD increased the tensile strength (TS) (by up to 27.6%) and elastic modulus (EM) (by up to 61.5%) of the CMC film. The CQD-added CMC films showed excellent antioxidant and strong antimicrobial activity. Lemon fruits were coated with the film solutions to test the efficacy of the CQD-added CMC film. The lemon coated with CMC/CQD film showed an excellent appearance without mold growth even after 21 days of storage. The CMC/CQD coating film with superior functional properties has great potential in active food packaging applications.

head.ce:title: Carboxymethyl cellulose-based functional film integrated with chitosan-based carbon quantum dots for active food packaging applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A chitosan-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multifunctional properties were synthesized using a hydrothermal process. The CQDs were spherical with an average size of 7.8 ± 2.4 nm. The CQD showed strong antioxidant activity, potent antibacterial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogenes, and antifungal activity against A. niger and P. chrysogenum. In addition, the prepared CQD showed negligible cytotoxicity against L929 cells even at a concentration of 500 μg/mL. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)-based functional films were fabricated by adding various amounts of CQD. The CQDs were evenly dispersed in the polymer matrix to form a highly transparent UV-blocking film. The addition of CQD increased the tensile strength (TS) (by up to 27.6%) and elastic modulus (EM) (by up to 61.5%) of the CMC film. The CQD-added CMC films showed excellent antioxidant and strong antimicrobial activity. Lemon fruits were coated with the film solutions to test the efficacy of the CQD-added CMC film. The lemon coated with CMC/CQD film showed an excellent appearance without mold growth even after 21 days of storage. The CMC/CQD coating film with superior functional properties has great potential in active food packaging applications.

The carboxymethyl cellulose/carbon quantum dots coating showed high antimicrobial and antioxidant properties and increased the shelf life of lemons.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: When it comes to packaging fresh food such as fruits and vegetables, the biggest challenges are maintaining freshness and quality, ensuring food safety, and extending the shelf life of the food [1]. Consumer complaints most often occur when fresh products have an insufficient shelf-life [2]. In recent years, active packaging has been widely used throughout the food supply chain to maintain product quality, ensure safety, and extend shelf life [3]. One of the most important active packaging applications is using functional packaging materials added with functional substances to provide antibacterial and antioxidant functions to prevent microbial contamination and inhibit adverse chemical or biological changes in packaged foods to maintain quality and ensure safety [4,5]. Currently, the most commonly used method for manufacturing active packaging materials is to incorporate active ingredients into the base polymers of food packaging materials [6]. For this purpose, functional nanomaterials, essential oils, and physiologically active natural plant extracts are widely used to impart functionality to active packaging materials [7,8]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been introduced as an excellent material that can be applied to active food packaging as a sustainable, eco-friendly, and safe functional material [9].

CQDs are a new class of carbon materials that have received much attention in the past few years. CQDs are emerging as functional fillers for food packaging applications due to their attractive properties such as high solubility in water, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, chemical inertness, and a simple synthesis method [10]. In addition, excellent functional properties such as UV protection, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties have been reported recently [11–13]. Although many carbon sources have been used to manufacture carbon dots, CQDs should be manufactured using simple, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly raw materials [14,15].

Chitosan is the second most abundant biopolymer obtained from chitin by deacetylation reaction that can be used as a starting material for CQD synthesis [16]. An eco-friendly, facile, and efficient CQD synthesis method using hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan under mild reaction conditions has been developed [17,18]. CQDs obtained from chitosan precursors can provide antioxidant and antibacterial properties with negligible cytotoxicity due to the various surface functional groups such as -OH, COOH, and -NH2 groups [19,20]. These biocompatible materials with antibacterial and fluorescent properties are introduced as beneficial nanomaterials in a wide range of important biomedical applications [21,22].

Currently, in food packaging, research using biopolymers such as carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids as an alternative to non-degradable synthetic plastics is being actively conducted [23]. Since these biopolymer-based packaging materials have excellent release functions except for biodegradability and eco-friendliness, they are expected to be used in the field of active packaging. One of these biopolymers, carboxymethylcellulose (CMC), a water-soluble cellulose derivative with anionic carboxyl groups, is widely used in the manufacture of biodegradable packaging films because it is renewable, biodegradable, non-toxic, and abundantly available [4]. CMC also has an excellent film-forming ability and good gas barrier properties, forming very transparent and flexible films with moderate tensile strength [24]. Several studies have already reported that CMC can be an excellent choice for biopolymer-based composite films [25–27]. However, CMC's lack of functionality limits its large-scale and industrial use for active packaging applications, which must be addressed [28]. In general, a method for manufacturing a multifunctional packaging film by adding functional nanofillers is widely used [4,23,24]. To this end, active fillers that fulfill both antibacterial and antioxidant functions will be a valuable and economical solution for the food packaging industry [7,29]. CQDs with strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activity can impart multifunctional properties to the biopolymer-based packaging films. In addition, the presence of active functional groups on the surface through surface modification of CQDs can cause interfacial interactions between CQDs and the base polymer to improve physicochemical properties such as mechanical properties and UV blocking properties. However, when CQD is added to the polymer matrix, the moisture barrier properties of the packaging film can be decreased due to the high hydrophilicity of CQD, depending on the basic polymer and the intrinsic properties of CQD [9,30].

However, only a few studies on the production of functional films using carbon quantum dots have been performed [9,29,31,32]. In particular, chitosan-based carbon quantum dots with excellent functional properties are expected to be used as active fillers for biopolymer films to improve the functional and physicochemical properties of the film; but there have been no reports on manufacturing biopolymer-based nanocomposite films or coatings using chitosan-based carbon quantum dots for active packaging.

Therefore, the main objective of this study was to fabricate CMC-based films by incorporating CQDs as functional materials for active packaging applications. The prepared film was characterized using SEM, FTIR, and TGA, and properties such as surface color, UV-light barrier, mechanical, water vapor barrier, antioxidant activity, and antimicrobial properties were investigated. The film's effectiveness was tested through an edible coating of lemon fruit to extend the fruit's shelf life.

2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), 2,2-diphenyl-1 picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), chitosan (viscosity 200–800 cP at 1% acetic acid, MW: 190,000–310,000, 75–85% deacetylated), and potassium persulfate were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Acetic acid, ascorbic acid, and acetone (>96%) were procured from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). The filamentous fungal species Aspergillus niger (PTTC 5154) and Penicillium chrysogenum (PTTC 5031) were supplied by the mycology laboratory of the medicine department at the Shahid Beheshti University of Iran. Escherichia coli O157: H7 (ATCC 43895) and Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313) were purchased from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC, Seoul, Korea).

CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally following the method of Malmir et al. [18] with a slight modification. Briefly, 3 g of ascorbic acid was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water at room temperature, and 1.5 g of chitosan was dissolved in 30 mL of 2% acetic acid solution in a separate container with vigorous stirring. Then, the ascorbic acid solution was mixed with the chitosan solution to obtain a uniform solution. The mixed solution was transferred to a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor and reacted in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min and filtered through a membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, pore size: 0.20 μm, Whatman International Ltd., Maidstone, England). The filtrate was freeze-dried to obtain a brown powder of CQD and stored at 4 °C before further testing.

A transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-2100F, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used to determine the size and shape of CQDs. The particle size distribution and average particle size were determined using ImageJ software. The UV–Vis spectra of CQDs and chitosan were obtained using a spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer UV WinLab, Lambda 950) in the range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, CA, USA) to characterize the photoluminescence properties of CQDs. FT-IR analysis was performed using a TENSOR 37 attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (AT-FTIR) spectrophotometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of the prepared CQDs, MTT analysis was performed using L929 fibroblasts. For this, L929 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The prepared cell suspension was seeded at a density of 1.6 × 104 cells/well in 96-well plates and incubated for 24h. The cells were then treated with different concentrations of CQDs (100, 250, and 500 μg/mL) for 24 h. Then, 50 μL MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for an additional 4 h. The DMSO was added to remove formazan crystals, and the absorbance of each well was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (Synergy HTX, Biotek). Data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) from at least three independent experiments.

CMC-based composite films were prepared using a solution casting method, as shown in Scheme 1 . First, 4 g of CMC was added to 150 mL distilled water containing 1.2 g of glycerol (30 wt% based on biopolymer) and dissolved by heating at 90 °C for 30 min with vigorous stirring. Various concentrations of CQD (0.0, 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 wt% based on CMC) were well dispersed in the solution with vigorous stirring for 30 min. The film-forming solutions were cast on Teflon-coated glass plates (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried at 25 °C for two days. The dried films were peeled off the glass plates and conditioned in a humidity chamber at 25 °C and 50% RH for at least 72 h before further testing. The prepared films were named CMC, CMC/CQD1.0, CMC/CQD3.0, and CMC/CQD5.0 according to the CQD concentration, respectively.

The surface morphology of the film samples was observed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU 8010, Hitachi Co., Ltd., Matsuda, Japan) operated at an acceleration voltage of 5 kV. For this, a small portion of film specimens was sputter-coated with platinum, placed directly on the sample holder, and the upper surface of the films was observed.

The chemical structure of the films was evaluated using an attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (ATR-FTIR, TENSOR 37 Spectrophotometer with OPUS 6.0 software, Billerica, MA, USA). The spectra were collected at a resolution of 4cm−1 with 32 scans over the wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1.

The films' X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using an XRD diffractometer (DMAX-2500, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). The XRD patterns were recorded in 2θ = 5–90° at a scanning speed of 0.4°/min. The Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1541 nm) with a nickel monochromator operating at 40 kV and 30 mA was used.

The apparent color of the film samples was recorded using a colorimeter (Konica Minolta CR-400 Chromameter, Tokyo, Japan) [33]. A standard white color plate was employed as a reference. The Hunter color (L, a, and b) values of the film sample were determined; where L, a, and b-values denote ‘lightness’, ‘greenness/redness’, and ‘blueness/yellowness’, respectively. The total color difference (∆E) was calculated from the following formula:
∆ E =
∆
L 2
+ ∆
a 2
+ ∆
b 2
where ∆L, ∆a, and ∆b are the difference between each color value of the film specimen and the standard plate, respectively.
The films' UV-light barrier and transparency were evaluated by measuring light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660).

The film samples were cut into rectangular strips (25 mm × 150 mm) using a precision double blade cutter, and the thickness of the samples was determined in five random points of each strip using a hand-held digital micrometer (QuantuMike IP 65 Digital Micrometer, Mitutoyo Corporation, Kanagawa, Japan). The Mechanical properties of films, including tensile strength (TS), percentage elongation at break (EB), and elastic modulus (EM), were measured using an Instron universal testing machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, USA). The machine was operated in the tensile mode with the initial grip separation of 50 mm, load cell of 500 N, and cross-head speed of 50 mm/min [4].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) and water vapor permeability (WVP)
ce:section.ce:para: The film samples (30 mm × 100 mm) were put on the analyzer's horizontal Teflon-coated steel stage. Then, 10 μL of distilled water was dropped on the film surface using a micro-syringe, and the contact angle was immediately measured using a WCA analyzer (Phoenix 150 Water Contact Angle Analyzer, Surface Electro-Optics Co. Ltd., Gunpo, Korea).

The films' water vapor permeability (WVP) was measured according to the ASTM E96–95 standards [34]. The film samples (75 mm × 75 mm) were sealed on top of the WVP cup containing 18 mL of distilled water, and the assembled cups were weighed and kept in a humidity chamber (25 °C and 50% RH). The weight change of the cups was recorded at regular intervals of 1 h for a period of 8 h, and the WVP (g.m/m2.Pa.s) of the film was calculated as follows:
WVP =
∆ w × L
∆ t × A × ∆ p
where w was the weight of the WVP cup (g), t was the time (s), A was the film area of the water vapor permeation (m2), L was the film thickness (m), and Δp was partial water vapor pressure difference across the two sides of the film (Pa).

The antioxidant activity of the films was evaluated by ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging assays [33]. Briefly, about 50 mg of film samples were added 10 mL of the DPPH (0.04 g/L in methanol) and ABTS (2.45 mM of ABTS solution prepared by mixing 7 mM ABTS solution and potassium sulfate) solutions and kept in the dark at ambient temperature for 30 min, and read the light absorbance at 517 and 734 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, respectively. The percentage of radical scavenging activity of the samples was calculated as follows:
Antioxidant activity
%
=
A c
−
A s
A c
× 100
where Ac and As are absorbances of ABTS or DPPH solutions for the control and sample solutions, respectively.

The test bacteria, E. coli and L. monocytogenes, were aseptically inoculated into 20 mL of TSB and BHI broth, respectively, and incubated at 37 °C for 16 h. 200 mg of each film sample was placed into a 100 mL conical flask containing 20 mL of the diluted inoculum of the test bacteria (105–106 CFU/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h with mild shaking at 100 rpm. The same diluted broth without the film sample was used as a control for comparison. Samples were taken at every 3 h interval, plated on an agar plate after serial dilution, and counted total viable colony (Log CFU/mL) [35].

The agar well diffusion test was used to measure zones of the antifungal activities of films according to document M51-A2 of the Clinical Laboratory Standard Institute. The filamentous fungal species (Aspergillus niger and Penicillium chrysogenum) were donated by the mycology laboratory of the medicine department at the Shahid Beheshti University of Iran. Microbial cultures were inoculated on potato dextrose agar (PDA; Merck, Germany) before incubation at 25 °C for 5–7 days. Petri dishes (diameter, 80 mm) were prepared with a PDA medium (15 mL). Fungal spores were collected and resuspended with sterile distilled water to obtain 106 spores/mL using a flow-cytometer. 100 μL suspension was spread on PDA media in Petri dishes. Wells were bored aseptically in the center of the media in the Petri dishes, having a diameter of 5 mm, and 100 μL of the film-forming solutions of each type was injected. The Petri dishes were then sealed with Parafilm® and incubated at 25 °C for three days. Then, the diameter of the inhibition zone was measured. The assays were performed in triplicate, and the experiment was repeated three times.

SEM imaging of the fungal strains was carried out to observe the change in the fungal morphology on exposure to the films. For this, the fungal culture in the 24 h exposed flask and the control flask were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to precipitate the mycelia, and the media was removed. The precipitated mycelia were washed three times with 0.1% peptone water and then resuspended in fresh peptone water. 50 μL of the culture was taken from each sample and drop cast on sterilized glass slides. The cells were fixed with 5% glutaraldehyde solution and washed twice with each of the 25%, 50%, 75% acetone aqueous solutions and finally with 100% acetone. Lastly, the slides were dried for at least 2 h at 35 °C in a hot air oven before imaging.

Terephthalic acid (TPA) was used to determine the hydroxyl radical formation capability of CMC-based nanocomposite films under visible light irradiation [36]. For this, an aqueous NaOH solution (2 mM) containing 0.5 mM TPA was prepared, and 0.005 g of the film samples was added to 30 mL of this solution and placed under a photoreactor equipped with white LED. After filtration, the 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTPA) concentration was recorded by observing the fluorescence emission peak at 430 nm under the excitation of 325 nm every 10 min of exposure to visible light.

The ability of the generation of O2 − of the CMC-based nanocomposite films was recognized by the oxidative bleaching 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a O2 − quencher under visible light [37]. In brief, 100 μL of DPBF solution in DMF (10 mM) was added to 30 mL of film sample suspension (0.005 mg/mL) and then placed under visible light (LED). About 3 mL solutions were taken every 1 min, filtered, and then recorded the PL spectrum under a 365 nm excitation wavelength.

The actual antimicrobial performance of nanocomposite films was evaluated to prove the application of the CQD-base films or coating in extending the shelf life of fresh fruits. The lemon, one of the fruits that are prone to mold, was chosen and coated to evaluate the antimicrobial effect of CQD. Fresh lemons were directly immersed in the film solutions for 2 min and dried at room temperature. The results of antibacterial and antifungal tests showed that 3% CQD is enough to inhibit the growth of microbes. Therefore, the uncoated and coated fruits with neat CMC and CMC/CQD3.0 solutions were put in a tray, stored under room humidity at 25 °C, and observed daily for any visible changes or fungal growth on their surfaces over 21 days. The experiment was repeated in triplicates.

All antimicrobial tests were performed in triplicate. Three replicated measurements were done with individually prepared films, and the results were presented as mean ± standard deviation. All data were analyzed using analysis of variance (ANOVA), and the significance of each mean value was evaluated (p < 0.05) by Duncan's multiple range test using a statistical analysis system (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the CQD and films

ce:section.ce:para: The chitosan-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were spherical in the 3–11 nm size range with an average of 7.8 ± 2.4 nm as confirmed by FE-TEM (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), and the CQD was covered with hydrophilic polar functional groups such as NH2, OH, COOH as verified by FTIR (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The CQD showed a clear color change from tan to green under UV light and exhibited tunable photoluminescence properties (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). The CQD showed strong antioxidant activity in DPPH and ABTS assays with negligible cytotoxicity towards L929 fibroblast cells even at a concentration of 500 μg/mL (Figs. S4 and S6, Supporting Information). In addition, the CQD exhibited potent antibacterial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogenes and antifungal activity against A. niger and P. chrysogenum (Fig. S5 and Table S1, Supporting Information). Details about the characteristics and properties of the CQD are described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Appearance and morphology of the films
ce:section.ce:para: All prepared films were flexible and free-standing. The appearance of the CMC-based films is shown in Fig. 1a. The neat CMC film and CQD-added CMC films were highly transparent, indicating that the CQD was uniformly dispersed in the CMC film. The neat CMC film was colorless, but the CQD-added film showed a light yellow color, and the color changed depending on the CQD concentration.

The surface morphology of CMC-based films was observed using SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 1b. All films showed a dense surface with no cracks or aggregations due to the homogeneous dispersion of CQDs in the polymer matrix. However, the CQD-added film showed a slightly rougher surface than the neat CMC film. The SEM results showed that CQD was uniformly dispersed in the CMC film, indicating good compatibility between CQD and CMC, mainly due to the hydrophilicity of CQD.

Chemical bonding and compatibility between CMC and CQD were investigated using FTIR analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 2a. A broad peak found at 3257 cm−1 was due to the OH stretching of CMC. The peak at 2920 cm−1 was assigned to the CH stretching vibration of the CH2 groups of the polymer. Peaks observed at 1593 and 1416 cm−1 correspond to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxyl group of the polymer, respectively. The CO bond stretching of the CMC structure was confirmed by an absorption band ranging from 1102 to 1020 cm−1 [38]. The CQD-added CMC film showed a similar FTIR pattern to that of the CMC film, except for a slight change in peak intensity, indicating no change in the chemical structure of the polymer matrix due to the addition of CQD. The slight decrease in the peak intensity of the composite film may be due to physical interactions (van der Waals interactions and H-bonds) between the CQD and the biopolymer [4].

XRD analysis was used to investigate the degree of change in crystallinity of CMC according to the addition of CQD, and the results are shown in Fig. 2b. CMC's characteristic broad diffraction peak was observed at 2θ = 22° for both the neat and CQD-added CMC films, suggesting that the addition of CQD did not generate a new diffraction peak in the CMC film. The crystallinity of CMC was not affected by the addition of CQD, probably due to the low concentration and uniform distribution of the added CQD.

The surface color and light transmittance of the CMC-based films are shown in Table 1 . The neat CMC film was transparent without color. The addition of CQD slightly decreased the brightness (L-value) of the CMC film while increasing the redness (a-value) and yellowness (b-value) of the CMC, which was attributed to the brown color of the CQD. As a result, the film's total color difference (ΔE) was significantly increased by adding CQD.

The light transmittance of the film was measured at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660) to evaluate the UV-barrier and transparency properties of the film. The CMC film has relatively high UV and visible light transmittance, with the T280 and T660 of 61.1% and 84.2%, respectively. The addition of CQD significantly decreased the T280 depending on the concentration of CQD, indicating that the UV-barrier properties of the film increased significantly by the addition of CQD. The UV-blocking properties of the film increased by ~80%, 99%, and 100% by the addition of 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 wt% of CQD, respectively. On the other hand, the transparency (T660) of the film was not significantly changed by the addition of CQD. The increased UV-barrier property of the CQD-added films was due to the UV-light absorption by the CQD [39]. However, the addition of CQD did not reduce the T660, probably because the size of the CQD (<10 nm) was much smaller than the wavelength of light (660 nm), which did not interfere with the transmission of light. As a result, the addition of CQD significantly increased the UV blocking properties without sacrificing the transparency of the CMC film. The UV protection properties of the CQD-added film are useful to prevent discoloration and oxidation of packaged foods [40].

The thickness and mechanical properties of the CMC-based films are shown in Table 2 . Film properties such as gas barrier and mechanical properties are usually affected by film thickness [26]. The thickness of the CMC film was not significantly changed (p > 0.05) by the addition of CQD due to the small particle size and uniform dispersion of CQDs in the CMC matrix.

In general, the mechanical properties (TS, EB, and EM) of composite films depend on the type of polymer matrix, the type and content of fillers used, the distribution of nanoparticles within the polymer matrix, and the compatibility between them [25,41]. The addition of CQD significantly increased the TS (by up to 27.6%) and EM (by up to 61.5%) of the CMC film, but the EB of the film decreased slightly. These results indicate that the CMC film becomes stronger and stiffer with reduced flexibility by adding CQD [34]. Previously, it was observed that the mechanical properties of biopolymer films such as gelatin and pullulan/chitosan films were reduced by the addition of high concentrations (>3%) of nanoparticles such as TiO2 and halloysite, mainly due to the aggregation of the nanoparticles [42,43]. However, in the present study, the mechanical strength of the CMC film was increased even when CQD was added up to 5 wt%, which can be attributed to the uniform distribution of CQDs in the polymer matrix due to their small and hydrophilic properties, as confirmed by the SEM and FTIR results. Besides, the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of CQDs and CMC can enhance the interaction between the CQD and the polymer, increasing the film strength through the better load distribution of the polymer due to strain-induced alignment [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) and water vapor permeability (WVP)
ce:section.ce:para: The WCA and WVP of biopolymer films are used to determine the film's hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity and water vapor barrier properties, respectively [46]. The WCA and WVP of the CMC-based films are also shown in Table 2.

The WCA of the neat CMC film was 38.2 ± 2.5°, indicating that the CMC film has a hydrophilic surface (WCA<65°) [47]. The WCA of the CMC film was significantly increased (p < 0.05) by the addition of CQD, and the degree of increase was dependent on the CQD content. The hydrogen bonds formed between the polymer and the CQD can reduce the hydroxyl groups in the CMC structure and thus reduce the surface hydrophilicity, increasing the WCA [26].

The WVP of the neat CMC was 1.42 × 10−9 g.m/m2.Pa.s and decreased significantly by the addition of CQD. The decrease in WVP of the CMC film depended on the CQD concentration, and when 5 wt% of CQD was added, it decreased to 1.29 × 10−9 g.m/m2.Pa.s. Although CQDs are hydrophilic, the uniform distribution of the polymer matrix creates a tortuous path for water vapor diffusion, reducing the WVP of the composite film [47]. The increased vapor barrier properties of the film are expected to prolong the shelf life of packaged products by preventing moisture transfer between the food and the outer packaging [48].

It is well known that adding antioxidants to food packaging is preferable to adding antioxidants directly to food to prevent deterioration of food quality due to the oxidation of fat components, nutrients, and pigments [49]. The antioxidant properties of CMC-based films were investigated using ABTS and DPPH methods, and the results are shown in Fig. 3a. The neat CMC film showed a slight antioxidant activity (8.1% for ABTS and 4.4% for DPPH), but the addition of CQD significantly increased the antioxidant activity of the CMC film. The antioxidant activity of the CQD-added CMC films increased as the concentration of CQD increased but did not further increase at 3 wt% or more. CMC films with CQD (3 and 5 wt%) showed 100% antioxidant activity in the ABTS method but 87 and 88% in the DPPH method. The higher antioxidant activity assessed by the ABTS method is attributed to the aqueous ABTS solution, resulting in better interactions between free radicals and hydrophilic CQDs.

On the other hand, the methanolic DPPH solution did not easily penetrate the hydrophilic CMC film. It did not readily interact with the hydrophilic CQD, showing lower antioxidant activity than the ABTS method. The antioxidant activity of the CQD can be attributed to the hydroxyl group located at the edge of the CQD structure, known as a hydrogen or electron donor for free radical scavenging [50,51]. The H transfer from the hydroxyl groups can reduce the ABTS and DPPH free radicals to form stable ABTS-H and DPPH-H assays. Therefore, biopolymers containing CQDs are expected to have a high potential for antioxidant packaging applications.

The release of CQD from the CMC-based films in water and alcohol solutions was evaluated, and the results are shown in Fig. 3b. The release rate of CQD is affected by the solubility and swelling properties of the polymer matrix and the type of food simulant solutions [33]. The release was initially rapid for all samples during the first 30 min and then decreased linearly and reached a plateau. The maximum release rate of CQD was obtained in 10% alcohol solution, followed by water, 50% ethanol, and 95% ethanol, indicating the CQD was released from the CMC-based film faster in water than the alcoholic solution. This release tendency can be attributed to the hydrophilicity of CQDs and the low solubility and swelling of CMC-based films in high alcohol solutions. The low swelling of the CMC-based film in 95% ethanol forms a closed structure and decreases the mobility and penetration of CQD, leading to the slow release of CQD [33]. The release test results explain the difference in antioxidant activity between the ABTS and DPPH methods of CMC-based films.

Antimicrobial packaging is often used to reduce food spoilage and food-related diseases caused by microbial growth or contamination during storage and transportation [3]. The antimicrobial activity of the CMC-based films was evaluated using a total colony count method against foodborne pathogenic bacteria, E. coli and L. monocytogenes, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . As expected, the neat CMC film did not show any antibacterial activity. In contrast, the CQD-added CMC films showed significant antibacterial activity against both types of bacteria, and the antibacterial activity increased as the concentration of CQD was increased. The CMC film incorporated with a low concentration of CQD (CMC/CQD1.0 film) showed bacteriostatic effect against both E. coli and L. monocytogenes; however, the CMC films with higher concentrations of CQD (3 and 5 wt%) showed a distinctive bactericidal effect. The CMC/CQD1.0 film retarded the growth of both test bacteria and showed almost 5 Log CFU/mL lower growth than the control or the neat CMC film at the end of the 12 h incubation. In contrast, the CMC/CQD3.0 film prevented the growth of E. coli and L. monocytogenes entirely after 9 h and 12 h of incubation, respectively. In particular, the CMC/CQD5.0 film showed strong antibacterial activity against both test bacteria and completely stopped growth after 3 h of exposure.

Antimicrobial packaging materials used to secure food safety and extend shelf life by preventing quality deterioration in the storage and distribution of fresh foods such as fruits and vegetables require antibacterial and antifungal activity [52]. The agar well diffusion method was used to determine the antifungal activity of the CMC-based films against A. niger and P. chrysogenum, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The neat CMC film showed no antifungal activity against both fungal strains. However, the CQD-added CMC films showed strong antifungal activity against A. niger and P. chrysogenum strains. The size of the fungal growth inhibition zone was proportional to the concentration of CQD added (Fig. 5b).

To confirm their antimicrobial activity, the morphological changes of bacteria (L. monocytogenes, E. coli) and mold (A. niger, P. chrysogenum) were investigated after treatment with CMC-based films using FE-SEM (Fig. 6 ). SEM images showed perfect cell structures of L. monocytogenes and E. coli when exposed to CMC films. However, the CMC/CQD film-treated groups showed some changes in morphological characteristics due to their antibacterial activity. Some damages such as gaps, shrinking, disorganization of the outer surface, and destruction of the bacterial cell walls were observed in the case of CQD-added films, depending on the CQD concentration. The mechanism of cell degradation can be attributed to the formation and penetration of ROS, leading to cell membranes collapsing and leakage of intracellular components.

Morphological, structural changes due to CMC/CQD films in fungi differ from those in bacteria. A typical fungal cell of A. niger and P. chrysogenum consists of several globular spores and hyphae, forming a branched filamentous structure similar to that of a plant. In general, conidia germinate to form new hyphae and ultimately give rise to macroconidia. The macroconidia then form new candida, and this continuum of cycles represents a normal fungal cell [53]. The FE-SEM image confirmed that the morphology of A. niger and P. chrysogenum cells after treatment with the CQD-added CMC films differed from that of the neat CMC film treated group (Fig. 6). In both fungi treated with the CQD-added CMC films, the fewer and smaller conidia with more and thinner hyphae indicate the damaged mycelial structure, which was intensified with the increasing carbon dot concentration. The morphological changes of the mycelial structure of A. niger and P. chrysogenum by the CQD-added CMC films confirm the film's antifungal activity (Fig. 5).

The antimicrobial function of CQDs is due to their small size and specific surface functional groups and the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). For antimicrobial substances to exhibit antimicrobial activity, they must first be absorbed into the bacterial cell wall either directly or through electrostatic interaction. Penetration of the agent into the cell wall causes leakage of intracellular components, protein destruction, chromosome condensation, and disruption of DNA replication [54]. Size significantly impacts the distribution of nanoparticles in the membrane structure, and ultra-small nanoparticles can easily diffuse through the cell wall [55]. Therefore, the quantum size of carbon dots can penetrate the cell wall of microorganisms.

CQDs can damage bacterial cells by creating intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) [56]. ROS results in the formation of free radicals such as hydroxyl free radicals (OH), and superoxide anions (O2 −) [57]. The ability of the CQD-added CMC films' free radicals (OH) formation was evaluated using a fluorescence method under visible light irradiation, and the results are shown in Fig. 7a. The non-fluorescent TPA (terephthalic acid) reacts with the photogenerated hydroxyl radicals, and the fluorescence intensity of the solution shows the formation of the fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTPA) [36,58]. Since the PL intensity is directly related to the amount of OH radicals, the higher fluorescence signal of CQD-added CMC films compared to the neat CMC film demonstrates the production of hydroxyl radicals in the solution.

The antibacterial and antifungal properties of the CMC-based composite films through the generation of O2 − can be described by reacting a colorimetric chemical trapping agent 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) with singlet oxygen and oxidative bleaching to form colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene [37]. The characteristic PL band intensity reduction at 485 nm provides evidence of the films' generation of O2 −. However, as shown in Fig. 7b, the neat CMC film did not generate ROS within 6 min. The emission intensity of DPBF at 485 nm of the CQD-added CMC films decreased as seen in the change in slopes of the line, confirming that the effective free radical generation was dependent on the CQD. The result that the generation of free radicals increased as the amount of CQD increased was in agreement with the result that the antimicrobial activity increased with the amount of CQD added.

The antimicrobial function of the CQD-added CMC film was evaluated through an edible coating test of fresh fruits. To evaluate the antibacterial effect of the CQD-added film, lemon, one of the fruits prone to mold growth, was selected. Fresh lemons were coated with different film solutions and stored under room conditions (25 °C, 50% RH) for 21 days, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . In the control group (uncoated), spoilage started after 7 days, and mold growth appeared on the lemon surface after 21 days. A similar trend can be seen in the samples coated with CMC solution, which is consistent with the antibacterial test results in which the neat CMC film did not show any antibacterial activity. On the other hand, lemons coated with the CMC/CQD3.0 film maintained their original taste and color, and no mold growth was observed on the lemon surface.

The fungal cell wall is the entry barrier for nanoparticles penetrating from the outside and the initial site of interaction with the nanoparticles. However, how nanoparticles pass through fungal cell walls and membranes is still not fully understood [59,60]. They can cause direct changes to the cellular structure and molecules inside fungal cells. Indirect effects include physical limitations (blocking effects), generation of reactive oxygen species, or solubilization of toxic nanoparticle compounds, depending on their properties (size, shape, concentration, morphology, and method of synthesis) [60,61]. As discussed in Section 3.3.2, in addition to the effect of size, reactive oxygen species such as (O2−) and (OH) are among the most important reasons for the antifungal action of CQDs. As a disproving fact, neat CMC showed no evidence of ROS production, and lemons coated with neat CMC had mold growth on the surface. On the other hand, CMC/CQD3.0 showed significant ROS production, and lemons coated with CMC/CQD3.0 inhibited mold growth. (Fig. 7).
The results show that the CQD-added CMC films can be used as edible coatings to extend the shelf life of fruits after harvest.

A chitosan-based carbon quantum dots with multifunctional properties were synthesized using a facile hydrothermal method. The size of the CQDs was 7.8 nm and was very stable in aqueous suspension. The CMC-based functional films were fabricated with various amounts of synthesized nanoparticles (1, 3, and 5 wt%). The SEM and FTIR test results showed homogenous dispersion of CQD in the polymer matrix and good compatibility between the CMC films and CQD due to the hydrophilic nature and small size of CQD. The CQD-added CMC film showed excellent UV barrier, high transparency, and improved mechanical and water barrier properties. Also, the CQD-added films showed excellent antioxidant and antibacterial and antifungal activities. Edible coating of lemon fruits with the CMC/CQD film showed an excellent effect in preventing mold growth and extending the shelf life of the fruit. The CQD-incorporated CMC films with excellent physical and functional properties are expected to be promising for active food packaging applications to extend the shelf life of fresh fruits.
